intro: The AI chip wars are heating up fast — SambaNova just pulled in a staggering $1 billion at an $11 billion valuation barely five months after its last mega-round, while French upstart ZML is taking a different swing at the infrastructure game with a free inference server designed to break chip vendor lock-in entirely. Beyond the hardware hustle, today's digest covers a clever new attack that turns AI coding assistants into botnet builders, Meta's controversial Muse Image launch that lets anyone AI-manipulate your Instagram photos without asking, and why San Francisco homes are selling for a million dollars over asking — because apparently the AI boom is reshaping everything, real estate included.

stories.title: Hot French startup ZML releases free product to speed inference across lots of AI chips
stories.summary: ZML, a French AI startup founded by former Zenly VP Steeve Morin and endorsed by Yann LeCun, has released ZML/LLMD, a free inference server that enables open-source large language models to run at maximum speed across multiple chip architectures including Nvidia, AMD, Google TPU, Apple Metal, and Intel Arc. The company aims to break vendor lock-in and reduce AI inference costs by allowing enterprises to use a mix of chips. ZML has raised $20 million from investors including 20VC, Kima Ventures, and LocalGlobe, and operates with a lean team of 20 people in Paris. The product competes with inference platforms like Baseten (valued at $13 billion), Inferact (from vLLM creators), and RadixArk (behind SGLang), but ZML claims broader ambitions including co-designing silicon with emerging European chipmakers. LLMD launches free to gather usage data before eventually becoming a paid product.
stories.opinion: This is a strategically smart move in a rapidly growing market. Inference optimization is indeed becoming more important than training as AI deployment scales, and chip-agnostic solutions address a real pain point around vendor lock-in and cost optimization. ZML's positioning is compelling — sitting at the intersection of the inference gold rush and the push for chip diversity beyond Nvidia. However, the competitive landscape is fierce, with well-funded competitors like Baseten and strong open-…

stories.title: AI chip maker SambaNova raises $1B at $11B valuation, 5 months after last mega round
stories.summary: AI chip maker SambaNova Systems has raised $1 billion at an $11 billion valuation in the first close of its Series F round, led by General Atlantic, with more investors expected soon. This comes just five months after a $350 million Series E and follows failed acquisition talks with Intel that valued the company at roughly $1.6 billion. CEO Rodrigo Liang said the company is likely headed toward going public but remains open to acquisition offers. Intel, a backer since Series C, participated in this round and has a multi-year co-development partnership with SambaNova. The company also announced JPMorganChase as an inference-infrastructure partner, deploying SN40L and SN50 systems for secure on-premises AI inference. SambaNova's next-generation SN50 chip, unveiled in February 2026, is set to begin shipping in the second half of 2026, with SoftBank as its first deployment partner. The comp…
stories.opinion: SambaNova's valuation leap from roughly $1.6 billion (Intel acquisition talks) to $11 billion in under a year is remarkable and reflects the intense investor appetite for AI infrastructure alternatives to Nvidia. The JPMorgan partnership is a significant validation, signaling that major financial institutions are serious about building private, on-premises AI inference capabilities rather than relying solely on cloud providers. However, the company's rapid fundraising pace raises questions abou…

stories.title: Hackers can use 9 of the most popular AI tools to assemble massive botnets
stories.summary: Researchers have discovered a new attack called 'HalluSquatting' that exploits AI coding assistants' tendency to hallucinate package and resource names. By predicting which non-existent package identifiers LLMs are most likely to fabricate and then registering those names in real repositories seeded with malicious code, attackers can create a scalable pull-based prompt injection attack. Unlike previous prompt injections that required targeting individual victims, HalluSquatting can indiscriminately compromise large numbers of devices as AI coding tools like Cursor, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Cline, and others automatically fetch and execute the malicious packages. This could enable massive botnets, large-scale DDoS attacks, ransomware campaigns, and cryptocurrency mining operations. The attack leverages the fundamental inability of LLMs to distinguish between real and halluci…
stories.opinion: This is a genuinely alarming and clever attack vector that represents a significant escalation in AI security threats. It elegantly combines two known problems — LLM hallucinations and dependency confusion/typosquatting — into something far more dangerous than either alone. The fact that it works against nine major AI coding tools suggests this is a systemic vulnerability rather than an implementation bug in any single product. What makes this particularly concerning is the scalability: unlike…

stories.title: Fax and Telegram? New York’s Archaic Requirements Get an A.I. Overhaul.
stories.summary: Governor Kathy Hochul is directing New York state agencies to eliminate or modernize outdated bureaucratic requirements, such as those involving fax machines and telegrams, using AI-driven approaches. The initiative also includes examining whether existing task forces or councils have outlived their purpose and should be disbanded or restructured.
stories.opinion: This is a sensible and overdue modernization effort. Many government agencies across the country still cling to archaic processes that waste time and resources. Using AI to identify and streamline outdated regulations is a practical application of the technology. However, the real test will be in execution — bureaucratic inertia is powerful, and simply directing agencies to modernize doesn't guarantee meaningful change. The review of task forces and councils is also welcome, as government bodie…

stories.title: ‘Absolutely bananas’: San Francisco homes sell for $1m above asking price amid AI boom
stories.summary: A Compass real-estate analysis found that in the first half of 2026, over 140 homes in San Francisco sold for at least $1 million above their asking price, with 44 of those occurring in June alone. The surge in overbidding is attributed to the city's AI boom, which has generated significant new wealth among tech workers and entrepreneurs, intensifying competition in an already housing-scarce market.
stories.opinion: This report highlights a familiar and troubling pattern in San Francisco: a tech-driven wealth surge colliding with chronic housing scarcity, producing extreme price distortions. While the AI boom is generating enormous economic value, the fact that homes routinely sell for $1 million or more above asking price underscores a deep structural failure in housing supply. Overbidding at this scale effectively locks out all but the wealthiest buyers and exacerbates inequality. San Francisco has long…

stories.title: Meta just launched a new AI generator, Muse Image, and users are already pushing back over use of their photos
stories.summary: Meta has launched Muse Image, a new AI image generator built by Meta Superintelligence Labs, available through the Meta AI app, Instagram Stories, and WhatsApp. The tool allows users to create AI-generated images, custom ads, interior decorating visualizations integrated with Facebook Marketplace, and prompt-based image editing. However, a controversial feature allows users to manipulate another Instagram user's public photos with AI by simply tagging them, without requiring explicit consent or notification. Users can disable this in settings, but it is enabled by default. Meta says the tool is free for everyday use but will require a subscription beyond certain limits. A video generation model called Muse Video is also in development.
stories.opinion: This launch exemplifies Meta's pattern of prioritizing engagement and feature adoption over user privacy and consent. The opt-out rather than opt-in approach to allowing strangers to manipulate your photos with AI is deeply problematic and feels like a deliberate design choice to maximize usage at the expense of user autonomy. While the decorating and advertising use cases are genuinely useful, the photo-tagging feature is a serious privacy concern that could enable harassment, deepfakes, and n…

stories.title: Meta’s new Muse Image model can pull other Instagram users into AI photos
stories.summary: Meta has launched its Muse Image model, the first AI image generation model from its Superintelligence Labs division, now powering image-making tools across Meta AI app, Instagram, and WhatsApp. The model is described as 'agentic,' working with Meta's Muse Spark large language model to reason through prompts, search the web, and plan before generating images. A notable feature allows users to @ mention other Instagram accounts in prompts, enabling the AI to incorporate those users' likenesses using their public photos. Users can also transform images, redesign rooms based on Facebook Marketplace listings, and draw directly on photos to make AI-powered changes. Meta is also planning a Muse Video model and rolling out 30 new AI effects for Instagram Stories in the US.
stories.opinion: The ability to @ mention other Instagram users and have their likeness pulled into AI-generated images is a deeply concerning feature, even if Meta claims users can control how their content is reused. In practice, most users won't understand or adjust these settings, effectively making their public photos fair game for anyone to generate AI imagery of them in any context. This feels like a significant erosion of personal image rights dressed up as a fun social feature. The broader trend of Met…

stories.title: Why the rise of open source AI isn’t hurting Anthropic … yet
stories.summary: The article explores why open source AI models haven't significantly hurt frontier AI labs like Anthropic despite their growing adoption. Citing Decagon CEO Jesse Zhang's theory, it argues that frontier and open source models serve two different phases of the same life cycle: expensive frontier models are used to prove out new use cases, which then migrate to cheaper open source alternatives as they mature. Data from Vercel and OpenRouter supports this, showing that while DeepSeek and other open source models lead in token volume, Anthropic still accounts for over half of total AI spending on these platforms. The article suggests this two-tiered economy — where frontier labs own discovery and open source owns production — may become a stable feature of the AI market, as the growing universe of AI-addressable tasks keeps demand for frontier models strong even as mature use cases shift to…
stories.opinion: This is a thoughtful and well-structured analysis of a genuinely important dynamic in the AI industry. The life cycle framing — frontier models for discovery, open source for production — is elegant and rings true based on the data presented. However, the article's own hedging ('yet') is warranted. The stability of this arrangement depends on frontier labs continuously generating enough novel, high-difficulty use cases to justify their premium pricing, which is not guaranteed as open source mod…

However, the competitive landscape is fierce, with well-funded competitors like Baseten and strong open-…

Commands for repeatable workflows, rules for code standards, skills for auto-triggered automation, agents for isolated subagents, and settings for permissions. 12+ config https://t.co/jGqAirg401 https://t.co/xp9tklh2tm

tweet_headlines.author: s4yonnara
tweet_headlines.headline: An ex-Google engineer breaks down how to give agents __real memory in three layers__: procedural for skills, semantic for durable facts, episodic for dated context.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Most AI agents forget everything the second you close the tab. This 12-minute breakdown fixes that.

An ex-Google engineer shows how to give agents real memory in three layers.

Procedural for how to act, your skills. Semantic for durable facts, the profile. Episodic for dated https://t.co/CQI74UVAGA https://t.co/9v2JLjvIuT
